answer.rationale: Paul's lexical pattern at Rom 3:28, Gal 2:16, Gal 3:11, Phil 3:9, and Eph 2:8-9 places dikaioo (G1344) through pistis (G4102) and apart from erga nomou (G2041/G3551) as the instrumental ground of right standing; logizomai (G3049) at Rom 4:3-5 frames the reckoning as gift, not wages. James 2:24 uses dikaioo in a demonstrative sense (faith proven by works), not as a contradicting ground.
answer.notes: Core grace-through-faith pattern is canonical and pan-Pauline. The 'alone' qualifier carries Reformation-confessional specificity that James 2:24 explicitly addresses by name (faith ou monon, not alone) using dikaioo in a vindicatory/demonstrative sense. Catholic and Orthodox lineages affirm justification by grace through faith while rejecting the Reformed 'alone' as worded; they emphasize cooperation with grace and works as fruit-but-not-mere-fruit. The doctrine clears canonical demonstration of the substance (grace-through-faith, not by works of the law) but the precise Reformed mechanism remains an in-house Christian dispute, not a gospel-denial boundary.

stem_audit.neutralized_form: A sinner is reckoned righteous before God on the basis of faith in Christ rather than on the basis of works of the law; what role works play in or after justification is the point under examination.
stem_audit.notes: The original statement uses Reformed-confessional vocabulary (ground of justification, fruit not basis) that assumes the Reformed answer to the very question James 2 reopens. Worked the neutralized form.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Scripture teaches that a person is set right with God on the basis of trust in Christ, not on the basis of keeping the law's requirements. The strongest reason for this is Paul. In Romans 3:28 he writes that a person is justified by faith apart from works of the law. In Romans 4:3-5 he says Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness, and that to the one who does not work but trusts the God who justifies the ungodly, faith is counted as righteousness. Ephesians 2:8-9 puts it most directly. By grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not your own doing, it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. Philippians 3:9 contrasts a righteousness of one's own from the law with the righteousness from God that depends on faith. The strongest complicating text is James 2:14-26. James asks whether a faith without works can save, says faith without works is dead, points to Abraham being justified by works when he offered Isaac, and says explicitly that a person is justified by works and not by faith only. The same Greek word for justified is used by both Paul and James. The tension is real at the surface and was felt strongly in church history. The most careful handling reads Paul and James as addressing different questions with the same word. Paul asks how a guilty sinner first receives right standing before God, and answers by faith, since works of the law cannot earn it. James asks how a claim to have faith is shown to be true and not empty, and answers by works, since a faith that produces no obedience is dead. On this reading Abraham was reckoned righteous in Genesis 15:6 when he believed (Paul's point) and was vindicated as a man of true faith decades later when he offered Isaac in Genesis 22 (James's point). The verdict therefore stands. Salvation is by grace through faith and not by works as the basis, but a living faith will inevitably produce works, and a faith that produces no works is not living faith at all.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Christian lineages broadly agree that salvation is by grace through faith and not by human merit, but they disagree sharply on the word alone and on what works contribute after the initial reception of grace. Lutherans and Reformed and most Anglicans and classical Methodists confess justification by faith alone as a central article of the Reformation. The Augsburg Confession article four and the Anglican Article 11 and the Methodist Article 9 all state that we are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of Christ by faith and not for our own works. The Roman Catholic Church teaches that justification comes from the grace of God as free and undeserved help, but rejects the Reformation slogan alone in the sense of excluding cooperation with grace. The Council of Trent in 1547 anathematized those who teach faith alone in a sense that requires no other cooperation. The 1999 Joint Declaration between Catholics and Lutherans reached a qualified shared statement that the sinner is justified by faith and through the grace of Christ, while both sides kept their distinct emphases. Eastern Orthodox lineages reject the term sola fide as a Western legal framing and prefer to speak of salvation as a lifelong synergy of grace and human response, with theosis as the goal. Pentecostal and most Baptist and Plymouth Brethren bodies affirm the Reformation position. Public-record cults that deny grace-through-faith and demand a works-based requirement for salvation include the Latter-day Saints (faith plus ordinances plus continued worthiness) and various legalistic groups (Iglesia ni Cristo's exclusive-church requirement, certain Branhamite teachings). The core grace-through-faith pattern is held across historic Christianity; the precise word alone is contested between Reformation heirs on one side and Rome and the East on the other.

scripture.key_term: δικαιοῦσθαι (dikaiousthai) G1344, πίστει (pistei) G4102, ἔργων νόμου (ergon nomou) G2041/G3551
scripture.force: Paul reckons that a person is justified by faith apart from works of the law; instrumental dative pistei contrasts with the excluded ground works of the law.

scripture.key_term: ἐπίστευσεν (episteusen) G4100, ἐλογίσθη (elogisthe) G3049, δικαιοσύνην (dikaiosynen) G1343
scripture.force: Abraham believed and it was reckoned to him as righteousness; logizomai is an accounting term for crediting to one's ledger.

scripture.key_term: λογίζεται (logizetai) G3049, χάριν (charin) G5485, ὀφείλημα (opheilema) G3783
scripture.force: To the worker, wages are not credited as a gift but as a debt; to the one who does not work but believes, faith is credited as righteousness.

scripture.key_term: δικαιοῦται (dikaioutai) G1344, πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (pisteos Iesou Christou) G4102
scripture.force: A person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ; the genitive permits objective (faith in) or subjective (faithfulness of) readings, both excluding works of the law as ground.

scripture.key_term: δικαιοῦται (dikaioutai) G1344, νόμῳ (nomo) G3551, πίστεως (pisteos) G4102
scripture.force: No one is justified before God by the law, for the righteous shall live by faith (citing Hab 2:4).

scripture.key_term: χάριτι (chariti) G5485, σεσῳσμένοι (sesosmenoi) G4982, πίστεως (pisteos) G4102, δῶρον (doron) G1435
scripture.force: By grace you have been saved through faith; this is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast.

scripture.key_term: δικαιοσύνην (dikaiosynen) G1343, νόμου (nomou) G3551, πίστεως (pisteos) G4102
scripture.force: Not a righteousness from law but the righteousness from God that depends on faith.

scripture.key_term: ἔργων (ergon) G2041, ἔλεος (eleos) G1656, ἔσωσεν (esosen) G4982, παλιγγενεσίας (palingenesias) G3824
scripture.force: He saved us not because of works done by us in righteousness but according to his own mercy; works excluded as ground.

scripture.key_term: אָמַן (aman) H539, חָשַׁב (chashav) H2803, צְדָקָה (tsedaqah) H6666
scripture.force: Abram believed Yahweh and he reckoned it to him as righteousness; the Hebrew lexical pattern that Paul cites in Rom 4 and Gal 3.

scripture.key_term: צַדִּיק (tsaddiq) H6662, אֱמוּנָה (emunah) H530, יִחְיֶה (yichyeh) H2421
scripture.force: The righteous shall live by his faith/faithfulness; load-bearing Hebrew text cited by Paul at Rom 1:17, Gal 3:11, and Heb 10:38.

scripture.key_term: δικαιοῦται (dikaioutai) G1344, ἔργων (ergon) G2041, πίστεως (pisteos) G4102, μόνον (monon) G3440
scripture.force: A person is justified by works and not by faith alone; the same verb dikaioo as Paul, but in a demonstrative/vindicatory sense (shown to be righteous), exemplified by Abraham offering Isaac after Gen 15:6.

scripture.key_term: πίστις (pistis) G4102, ἔργα (erga) G2041, νεκρά (nekra) G3498
scripture.force: Faith by itself if it does not have works is dead; complicates only the empty profession reading of sola fide, not the gift-of-grace ground.

scripture.key_term: δικαιωθήσονται (dikaiothesontai) G1344, ποιηταί (poietai) G4163, νόμου (nomou) G3551
scripture.force: Not the hearers of the law but the doers will be justified; appears to qualify Rom 3:28, generally read as eschatological vindication contingent on the same faith-wrought obedience.

scripture.key_term: δικαιωθήσεται (dikaiothesetai) G1344, ἔργων νόμου (ergon nomou) G2041/G3551
scripture.force: By works of the law no flesh will be justified in his sight; the negative twin of Rom 3:28.

scripture.key_term: δικαιωθέντες (dikaiothentes) G1344, πίστεως (pisteos) G4102, εἰρήνην (eirenen) G1515
scripture.force: Having been justified by faith, we have peace with God; aorist participle treats the justification as a settled completed event grounded in faith.
Everyone who believes is justified from everything from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses; Paul's preaching at Pisidian Antioch ties faith to release from law as ground.
The tax collector went down to his house justified rather than the Pharisee; Jesus pictures justification on the basis of a self-emptying cry for mercy, not Torah-keeping.

competing_lens_verdicts.verdict: affirms sola fide as worded; dikaioo is forensic-declarative; Paul and James use dikaioo in two senses
complicates; works of the law refers primarily to Jewish boundary markers (circumcision, food laws, sabbaths), reframing what sola fide excludes
affirms by grace through faith; denies alone as worded; reads James 2:24 as integral, not subordinate, to Pauline justification
denies sola fide as worded; affirms grace-initiated cooperation; canon 9 of Trent Session 6 anathematizes faith alone in a sense that excludes cooperation

hermeneutics.notes: The verbal harmonization of Paul and James turns on whether dikaioo carries the same lexical sense in both. Paul's accounting-language (logizomai) and contrast with works of the law point to a forensic declaration. James's flow (faith proves itself by works, Abraham vindicated by offering Isaac decades after Gen 15:6) reads naturally as demonstrative justification. The Reformation slogan as worded carries this exegetical commitment; the substance (grace-through-faith, not by works of the law) is canonically demonstrable on a wider lexical base.

counter_witness.key_phrase: are freely justified for Christ's sake, through faith, when they believe that they are received into favor

counter_witness.anchor: United Methodist Articles of Religion, Article IX, https://www.umc.org/en/content/articles-of-religion
counter_witness.key_phrase: We are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, by faith, and not for our own works or deservings

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church on grace and justification (CCC 1996), via vatican.va
counter_witness.key_phrase: Our justification comes from the grace of God. Grace is favor, the free and undeserved help that God gives us to respond to his call

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles, Article XI Of the Justification of Man (historic public text)
counter_witness.key_phrase: We are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ by faith, and not for our own works or deservings

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith, Chapter 11 On Justification
counter_witness.key_phrase: Faith, thus receiving and resting on Christ and His righteousness, is the alone instrument of justification

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America doctrinal materials, oca.org; representative Orthodox theological position
counter_witness.key_phrase: Salvation is by grace, received through faith, and worked out in a lifelong synergy with God's grace; the Western legal framing of sola fide is not the Orthodox framework
If anyone says that by faith alone the sinner is justified, so as to mean that nothing else is required to cooperate, let him be anathema

web.quote: dikaiousthai G1344 present passive infinitive 'to be justified'; pistei G4102 instrumental dative 'by faith'; ergon nomou G2041/G3551 'works of law' excluded
pisteos Iesou Christou genitive permits both objective ('faith in') and subjective ('faithfulness of') readings; both exclude works of law as ground
chariti G5485 dative 'by grace', sesosmenoi G4982 perfect passive 'have been saved', pisteos G4102 'through faith', doron G1435 'gift'
elogisthe G3049 aorist passive 'was reckoned/credited'; accounting metaphor of faith placed on the ledger as righteousness
dikaioutai G1344 with monon G3440 'a person is justified by works and not by faith alone'; demonstrative/vindicatory sense in context
freely justified for Christ's sake, through faith, when they believe that they are received into favor
accounted righteous before God only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, by faith, and not for our own works or deservings
